questions.question: An industrial plant can produce either 60 custom engines or 180 standard turbines. The plant manager decides to produce 15 additional custom engines. How many standard turbines must be removed from the production schedule?
options: 15 standard turbines, matching the increase in custom engine output
45 standard turbines, reflecting the opportunity cost of the new engines
30 standard turbines, half of the total turbine production capacity
60 standard turbines, equal to the entire custom engine production line
questions.explanation: Opportunity cost of 1 custom engine = 180 ÷ 60 = 3 turbines. For 15 additional engines, 15 × 3 = 45 turbines must be sacrificed.

questions.question: Country North produces a unit of timber in 4 hours and a unit of iron in 8 hours. Country South produces timber in 6 hours and iron in 10 hours. Which country has the absolute advantage in iron production?
options: Country South, because its timber production takes fewer hours than its iron
Country North, because it requires fewer labor hours to produce a unit of iron
Country South, because its iron productivity is closer to its timber productivity
Neither country, because absolute advantage requires superiority in both goods
questions.explanation: Country North needs 8 hours for iron vs. Country South's 10 hours. Fewer hours per unit means higher absolute productivity in iron.

questions.question: Sweden can produce 50 units of furniture or 100 units of paper. Finland can produce 40 units of furniture or 120 units of paper. Which country should specialize in furniture according to comparative advantage?
options: Finland, because it produces more paper than Sweden in absolute terms
Sweden, because its opportunity cost of furniture is lower than Finland's
Finland, because its furniture output per worker is growing faster
Sweden, because it can produce both goods more efficiently than Finland
questions.explanation: Sweden's opportunity cost for furniture is 2 paper (100 ÷ 50). Finland's is 3 paper (120 ÷ 40). Sweden has the lower relative cost.

questions.question: A nation is currently producing at a point inside its Production Possibility Frontier. What does this indicate about its resource utilization?
options: The nation has reached its maximum possible output of both goods
The nation is experiencing unemployment or underutilization of resources
The nation is producing an unattainable combination beyond its capacity
The nation has achieved both productive and allocative efficiency
questions.explanation: Points inside the PPF are attainable but inefficient. The economy could produce more of both goods without increasing total resources.

questions.question: Why does the absence of trade under autarky limit a nation's consumption possibilities to equal its production possibilities?
options: Government regulations prevent consumers from buying more than is produced
Because the nation can only consume what it is capable of producing internally
Autarky prices are always too high for consumers to afford additional output
Production under autarky always occurs at a point inside the production frontier
questions.explanation: Without international exchange, the consumption frontier and production frontier are identical, limiting consumption to domestic output.

questions.question: There is a worldwide shift in consumer preferences toward Good B, with supply held constant. What happens to the global relative price of Good A?
options: The relative price of Good A rises as its relative scarcity increases
The relative price of Good A falls due to decreased relative demand
The relative price remains constant because supply has not changed
The relative price of Good A and Good B both increase proportionally
questions.explanation: A worldwide preference shift toward Good B reduces relative demand for Good A, shifting its demand curve left and lowering its equilibrium relative price.

questions.question: In a Ricardian model with two trading nations, where does the equilibrium relative price always fall on the world supply and demand diagram?
options: Exactly at the midpoint between the two nations' absolute advantage ratios
Between or at the domestic opportunity costs of the two trading nations
At the higher of the two domestic opportunity costs to maximize exporter profit
Below both domestic costs to ensure consumers in both nations benefit equally
questions.explanation: Trade will only occur at a price acceptable to both parties, which must fall within or at the boundaries of their internal cost ratios.

questions.question: The United States is twice as productive as Mexico in all sectors, but US wages are three times higher than Mexican wages. How will trade affect US manufacturing in the Ricardian model?
options: The US will export all goods because its productivity is higher in every sector
The US will likely import these goods because the wage gap exceeds the productivity gap
Mexico will be unable to export anything due to its lower absolute productivity
Both countries will produce everything domestically and no trade will occur
questions.explanation: Even with higher absolute productivity, if the wage gap (3:1) exceeds the productivity gap (2:1), the high-wage nation is at a cost disadvantage.

questions.question: A worker produces 5 units of Good X per hour and the price of Good X is \$10. According to the Ricardian model, what is the worker's hourly wage?
options: \$5 per hour, reflecting the marginal cost of producing each unit
\$50 per hour, equal to the value of the marginal product of labor
\$10 per hour, matching the price of a single unit of Good X
\$2 per hour, calculated as the price divided by the units produced
questions.explanation: In a competitive labor market, nominal wage = P × MPL = \$10 × 5 = \$50 per hour.

questions.question: Which key assumption of the Ricardian model justifies the use of a linear Production Possibility Frontier?
options: Increasing returns to scale that lower costs as production expands
Constant returns to scale and fixed unit labor requirements for each good
Perfect factor mobility between countries but not within each country
Diminishing marginal returns to labor in each of the two sectors
questions.explanation: Because the labor required per unit does not change with the scale of production, the trade-off ratio remains constant, producing a linear PPF.

questions.question: How does trade improve the real income of workers in a Ricardian economy?
options: By increasing nominal wages through higher demand for all types of labor
By reducing the price of imported goods relative to the workers' wages
By redistributing tariff revenues directly to workers through government transfers
By forcing firms to adopt more labor-intensive production techniques after trade
questions.explanation: While nominal wages are tied to the export sector, cheaper imports reduce the cost of living, raising real purchasing power for all workers.

questions.question: A trade agreement increases national wealth by \$500 million but displaces 5,000 workers. Under what condition does this satisfy the Kaldor-Hicks efficiency criterion?
options: If the government actually compensates every displaced worker for their losses
If the \$500 million gain could theoretically compensate all 5,000 workers' losses
If the number of jobs created elsewhere exceeds the 5,000 jobs that were lost
If the trade agreement is approved by a majority vote in a national referendum
questions.explanation: Kaldor-Hicks efficiency requires only that winners could potentially compensate losers, not that they actually do so.

questions.question: An economy is producing at a point on its PPF, but consumers prefer a different mix of goods than what is being produced. Is the economy Pareto Efficient?
options: Yes, because being on the PPF guarantees that no resources are being wasted
No, because utility could be increased by reallocating resources to match preferences
Yes, because production efficiency is the only requirement for Pareto Efficiency
No, because the economy must also be growing to achieve Pareto Efficiency
questions.explanation: Pareto Efficiency requires both production efficiency (being on the PPF) and allocative efficiency (producing what consumers actually want).

questions.question: In the Ricardian model, the price of the export good rises by 20% and the country is fully specialized. What happens to nominal wages?
options: Nominal wages rise by less than 20% because firms absorb part of the gain
Nominal wages rise by exactly 20%, moving in lockstep with the export price
Nominal wages remain unchanged because productivity has not increased
Nominal wages rise by more than 20% due to the Magnification Effect
questions.explanation: With all labor in the export sector and wages equal to P × MPL, nominal wages move in lockstep with the export price.

questions.question: Wages in the export sector rise due to increased global demand. What happens to wages in the domestic non-traded sector?
options: They remain unchanged because non-traded goods are isolated from global markets
They must also rise to retain labor, given perfect internal factor mobility
They decrease as workers leave the non-traded sector for higher export wages
They are set by government regulation and unaffected by market forces
questions.explanation: Perfect internal factor mobility forces wage equalization. Non-traded sector wages must rise or all workers will leave for the export sector.

questions.question: Labor mobility within a country is restricted by significant geographical barriers. How does this affect wage convergence after trade opening?
options: Wages converge faster because workers are forced to stay in their home regions
Regional wage disparities will persist because workers cannot move to growing areas
Wages equalize immediately through international migration instead of domestic moves
Geographical barriers have no effect because trade equalizes wages without mobility
questions.explanation: Mobility is the mechanism that equalizes wages. Without it, workers in regions lacking export industries do not benefit from trade-driven price increases.

questions.question: In the immobile factor model, the world price of a nation's export good suddenly drops. How are owners of capital in that export industry affected?
options: Their income falls significantly because the capital cannot move to other sectors
Their income rises because lower prices stimulate higher export sales volumes
Their income remains stable because capital returns are set by long-term contracts
Their income shifts to other sectors as the capital is quickly redeployed
questions.explanation: Because the capital cannot be relocated to a more profitable sector, its owners must absorb the full impact of the world price decline.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the short-run nature of the immobile factor model?
options: It assumes that all factors can move freely in response to price signals
It assumes insufficient time has passed for workers to retrain or relocate
It focuses exclusively on capital mobility while ignoring labor market dynamics
It analyzes periods of fifty years or more where all adjustments are complete
questions.explanation: The model focuses on immediate impacts before factors have had the opportunity to respond to changed market signals through retraining or relocation.

questions.question: In a world of immobile factors, why would a barber in the domestic service industry care about a tariff imposed on imported steel?
options: Steel tariffs directly increase the cost of scissors and razors used by barbers
Because tariffs affect the general price level and the barber's real purchasing power
The barber is unaffected because services are not traded in international markets
Tariffs on steel always lead to higher nominal wages in the service sector
questions.explanation: Even if the barber's nominal wage is unaffected, changes in the general cost of living driven by trade policy impact their real welfare.

questions.question: Country A has 400 workers and 100 machines. Country B has 1,000 workers and 200 machines. According to the H-O model, which country is relatively labor-abundant?
options: Country A, because it has fewer total machines than Country B possesses
Country B, because its labor-to-capital ratio of 5:1 exceeds Country A's 4:1
Country A, because a smaller total workforce indicates greater labor scarcity
Neither country, because both have more workers than machines in absolute terms
questions.explanation: Abundance is measured by ratios, not totals. Country B's ratio is 5:1 (1,000/200) vs. Country A's 4:1 (400/100), making B relatively labor-abundant.

questions.question: Textile production is labor-intensive and chemical production is capital-intensive. An increase in the price of textiles occurs. How will capital-to-labor ratios change in both industries?
options: The capital-to-labor ratio will fall in both as firms hire more workers
The capital-to-labor ratio will rise in both as wages increase more than rents
The ratio rises in textiles but falls in chemicals due to the output mix change
The ratios remain constant because factor intensity is technologically fixed
questions.explanation: The Stolper-Samuelson effect raises wages more than capital rents. Firms in both sectors respond by substituting capital for the now more expensive labor.

questions.question: What does the Heckscher-Ohlin model predict about the convergence of living standards between two trading nations?
options: Living standards will diverge as rich countries capture all the gains from trade
Factor prices like wages will equalize even without labor moving across borders
Only capital returns will converge while wage differences will persist permanently
Convergence requires both nations to adopt a common currency and central bank
questions.explanation: The Factor Price Equalization theorem states that trading goods serves as a substitute for trading the factors themselves, leveling wages and rents globally.

questions.question: A country's labor force grows significantly while its capital stock remains the same. According to the Rybczynski Theorem, how will its export mix change?
options: It will export more of both goods as total productive capacity expands
It will export more labor-intensive goods and fewer capital-intensive goods
It will export more capital-intensive goods as labor is substituted for capital
Its export mix remains unchanged because comparative advantage is unaffected
questions.explanation: An increase in one factor endowment expands the sector using that factor intensively and contracts the other, shifting the export mix accordingly.

questions.question: In the Specific Factor Model with Labor (mobile), Capital (specific to Tech), and Land (specific to Corn), what is the effect of immigrant labor on the real return to Tech Capital?
options: The real return to capital decreases as labor supply dilutes productivity
The real return to capital increases because more labor raises its marginal product
The real return is unaffected because capital returns are set in global markets
The real return falls for Tech Capital but rises for Land in the Corn sector
questions.explanation: More labor working with fixed capital increases the marginal productivity of that capital, raising the real return for capital owners.

questions.question: A 10% increase occurs in the price of both goods in a Specific Factor Model. Why does the allocation of labor remain unchanged?
options: Labor is immobile in the Specific Factor Model and cannot respond to prices
Because the relative value of the marginal product stays constant across sectors
Wages rise by 10% in one sector but fall by 10% in the other, canceling out
Government price controls prevent labor from moving between the two sectors
questions.explanation: Equal proportional price increases leave the relative Value of Marginal Product unchanged, neutralizing any incentive for labor to switch industries.

questions.question: In the Specific Factor Model, how is the equilibrium wage for the mobile factor determined?
options: By government minimum wage legislation applied uniformly across sectors
At the intersection of the Labor Demand curves for both sectors combined
By the average productivity of labor across all industries in the economy
At the point where wages equal the price of the most expensive good produced
questions.explanation: Labor demand in each sector equals the Value of Marginal Product. The equilibrium wage is where total labor demand equals total labor supply.

questions.question: A natural disaster destroys 20% of a country's agricultural Land in the Specific Factor Model. What happens to the nominal wage in Manufacturing?
options: The nominal wage increases as manufacturing becomes relatively more productive
The nominal wage decreases as displaced farm workers flood into manufacturing
The nominal wage remains unchanged because land and factories are unrelated
The nominal wage rises in the short run but falls back in the long run
questions.explanation: With less land, agricultural labor demand falls, pushing workers into manufacturing where the increased labor supply drives down wages.

questions.question: In a market with internal economies of scale, what happens to the price of the good as the number of firms in the industry increases?
options: The price rises because each firm produces less and has higher average costs
The price falls due to intensified competition among a larger number of firms
The price remains constant because it is set by the largest firm in the market
The price initially rises then falls as firms enter and exit the industry
questions.explanation: In the Krugman model, more firms intensify competition, reducing the price-marginal cost markup even if average costs change.

questions.question: What is agglomeration in the context of increasing returns and New Trade Theory?
options: The merger of multiple small firms into a single large multinational corporation
The clustering of similar firms in one location to capture external economies
The process of relocating production to countries with the lowest wage costs
The division of a production process into separate stages across many countries
questions.explanation: Agglomeration occurs when firms cluster geographically to share specialized labor pools, supplier networks, and benefit from knowledge spillovers.

questions.question: Why might a country that first produces a good with increasing returns remain the dominant exporter even if another country later becomes more naturally efficient?
options: International trade law grants permanent protection to first-moving industries
Due to the first-mover advantage, early scale makes later entry uncompetitive
Natural efficiency never actually changes once a country has established an industry
Governments always subsidize first movers to keep them permanently dominant
questions.explanation: Once a firm achieves scale, average costs fall so low that new entrants cannot compete regardless of their potential productivity advantage.

questions.question: A large country imposes an export subsidy on its own producers. How does this affect the world price of that good?
options: The world price increases because the subsidy raises production costs globally
The world price decreases as the subsidy expands global supply of the good
The world price remains unchanged because subsidies only affect domestic prices
The world price rises for large countries but falls for small exporting nations
questions.explanation: The subsidy increases global supply of the export good, pushing the international equilibrium price downward.

questions.question: Which trade policy is specifically designed to offset the price advantage created when a foreign government subsidizes its own exporters?
options: Voluntary export restraints that limit the volume of subsidized shipments
Countervailing duties that neutralize the effect of the foreign subsidy
Most-favored-nation tariffs applied equally to all trading partners
Import licensing requirements that screen goods for subsidy content
questions.explanation: Countervailing duties are tariffs specifically imposed to offset foreign government subsidies and level the playing field for domestic producers.

questions.question: Why is the deadweight loss from an import quota often higher than the deadweight loss from an equivalent tariff?
options: Quotas always restrict more imports than tariffs at the same nominal rate
If licenses are not auctioned, the quota rents may be lost to the domestic economy
Tariffs generate government revenue that can be used to offset their deadweight loss
Quotas cause larger production distortions because they are harder to administer
questions.explanation: Both cause production and consumption distortions, but quota rents may be wasted or captured by foreign firms instead of remaining in the domestic economy.

questions.question: According to the Median Voter Theorem, why do trade protections often persist even if they harm the majority of consumers?
options: The median voter is typically a worker in a protected import-competing industry
Benefits are concentrated among a few while costs are spread thinly across many
Consumers do not vote in elections while producers organize politically
Trade protections are required by international treaties that cannot be changed
questions.explanation: Small groups with concentrated gains have strong lobbying incentives, while individual consumers each lose too little to motivate organizing against protection.

questions.question: A tariff increases the domestic price from \$100 to \$110 and imports fall from 500 to 400 units. What is the government revenue collected?
options: \$500, calculated as the price increase times the original import volume
\$4,000, equal to the per-unit tariff times the new quantity of imports
\$1,000, reflecting the total price increase across all imported units
\$5,000, representing the tariff rate times the original import volume
questions.explanation: Government revenue = tariff per unit (\$110 – \$100 = \$10) × quantity of imports after the tariff (400) = \$4,000.

questions.question: How does a tariff affect the incentive for domestic import-competing firms to innovate?
options: It increases innovation by providing firms with more profits to fund research
It generally reduces the incentive by shielding firms from competitive pressure
It forces firms to innovate because they must compete with tariff-free exporters
Tariffs have no impact on innovation because technology is unrelated to trade
questions.explanation: Protection allows firms to earn profits without pursuing efficiency gains or technological upgrades, reducing the competitive pressure to innovate.

questions.question: An automobile industry has an Effective Rate of Protection of 50%. What does this percentage actually measure?
options: The nominal tariff rate applied to imported automobiles at the border
The percentage increase in industry value-added compared to free trade conditions
The share of domestic automobile production that is exported to foreign markets
The fraction of a car's components that must be sourced from domestic suppliers
questions.explanation: ERP measures how much more domestic firms can spend on labor and capital per unit of output while remaining competitive with foreign producers.

questions.question: Under what condition does a large country maximize its national welfare using a tariff?
options: When the tariff rate is set high enough to eliminate all imports completely
When the marginal terms-of-trade gain equals the marginal deadweight loss
When government tariff revenue is sufficient to eliminate the budget deficit
When the domestic price after the tariff equals the foreign autarky price
questions.explanation: The optimal tariff is found where the benefit from lower world prices is exactly offset by the cost of domestic production and consumption distortions.

questions.question: A regulation requires foreign car manufacturers to source 40% of components from domestic suppliers. What type of trade policy is this?
options: A voluntary export restraint limiting the foreign content of imported cars
A local content requirement mandating the use of domestic intermediate inputs
A tariff-rate quota with a lower rate for cars using domestic components
A countervailing duty offsetting foreign subsidies to component manufacturers
questions.explanation: Local content requirements force a specific level of domestic input use to boost local industry and employment in the supply chain.

questions.question: What is the primary difference between a Voluntary Export Restraint and an Import Quota under international trade law?
options: VERs apply to all countries while quotas target only specific exporting nations
VERs were traditionally used to bypass GATT rules but are now largely prohibited
Import quotas are always more restrictive than VERs at the same volume limit
VERs require compensation to exporters while quotas do not require payment
questions.explanation: VERs allowed countries to restrict trade without formally declaring a quota, which would have required compensation under GATT rules. They are now largely banned.

questions.question: How does an export tax imposed by a large country affect its terms of trade?
options: It worsens the terms of trade by making exports less competitive globally
It improves the terms of trade by restricting supply and raising world prices
It has no effect on terms of trade because taxes only affect domestic prices
It improves terms of trade only if other countries also impose export taxes
questions.explanation: By restricting export supply to the world market, a large country forces the world price of those exports to rise, improving its terms of trade.

questions.question: Which non-tariff barrier involves a government restricting its own purchases to domestic firms even when foreign suppliers submit lower bids?
options: Local content requirements that mandate domestic sourcing in all sectors
Government procurement policies that favor domestic over foreign suppliers
Import licensing systems that delay or deny foreign firms' applications
Technical barriers to trade that exclude foreign products on safety grounds
questions.explanation: Buy National government procurement policies act as an implicit subsidy and protection for domestic industry by closing off the public sector market.

questions.question: A country has GDP of \$1,000, Consumption of \$700, Government Spending of \$200, and Investment of \$200. What is the Current Account balance?
options: \$100 surplus, because the country is producing more than it is spending
–\$100 deficit, calculated as GDP minus the sum of consumption, investment, and government spending
\$0, because the accounts must always balance with the Financial Account
–\$300 deficit, reflecting the excess of investment over government spending
questions.explanation: Using GDP – (C + I + G) = CA: \$1,000 – (\$700 + \$200 + \$200) = –\$100, indicating a current account deficit.

questions.question: A country's Secondary Income account in the Balance of Payments includes which of the following transactions?
options: Exports of manufactured goods to foreign retail and wholesale markets
Remittances sent home by workers who are living and employed abroad
Interest payments received on foreign bonds held by domestic residents
Foreign direct investment by domestic firms into overseas subsidiaries
questions.explanation: Secondary income covers transfers where nothing is received in return, such as foreign aid, gifts, and private remittances from workers abroad.

questions.question: What is the economic significance of the Statistical Discrepancy item in the Balance of Payments?
options: It measures the difference between a country's exports and imports of services
It accounts for errors, omissions, and unrecorded capital flows in the data
It represents the change in the central bank's official foreign reserve holdings
It tracks the difference between nominal and real exchange rate movements
questions.explanation: Because BOP data is collected from many different sources, errors and omissions are inevitable. The statistical discrepancy forces the accounts to balance.

questions.question: How does a currency crisis typically manifest itself in a country's Balance of Payments?
options: A massive outflow in the financial account and a sudden depletion of reserves
A large and growing surplus in the current account as exports become cheaper
A gradual increase in foreign direct investment as assets become undervalued
A balanced current account offset by an equal and opposite financial account
questions.explanation: During a currency crisis, investors pull capital out rapidly, the financial account shows massive outflows, and the central bank depletes reserves defending the peg.

questions.question: The interest rate on a Dollar asset is 5% and the interest rate on a Euro asset is 2%. According to Interest Rate Parity, what is the expected appreciation of the Euro against the Dollar?
options: 7%, reflecting the sum of the two different interest rates on the assets
3%, equal to the difference between the Dollar and Euro interest rates
2%, matching the lower interest rate available on the Euro-denominated asset
5%, because the higher-yielding currency always depreciates by its own rate
questions.explanation: To equalize returns, the lower-interest currency (Euro at 2%) must be expected to appreciate by the interest differential (5% – 2% = 3%).

questions.question: What is capital flight in the context of international finance?
options: The gradual shift of investment from manufacturing to service industries
The rapid movement of large sums of money out of a country due to instability
The normal rebalancing of portfolios between domestic and foreign assets
The central bank's policy of selling domestic currency to buy foreign reserves
questions.explanation: Capital flight involves large-scale, rapid outflows of money driven by economic or political instability, putting intense downward pressure on the currency.

questions.question: Under a floating exchange rate system, what is the self-correction mechanism for a persistent trade deficit?
options: The government raises tariffs until imports fall enough to balance trade
The currency depreciates, making exports cheaper and imports more expensive
Interest rates automatically rise, reducing domestic consumption and imports
Foreign countries voluntarily reduce their exports to restore trade balance
questions.explanation: Market forces adjust the currency value downward, which increases export competitiveness and makes imports costlier, naturally rebalancing trade.

questions.question: What does the Law of One Price state in international economics?
options: All goods within a single country must sell for the same price in every city
Identical goods must sell for the same price in different markets when expressed in a common currency
The price of any good is determined solely by the cost of the labor used to produce it
Prices can only differ across countries by the exact amount of the transportation cost
questions.explanation: The Law of One Price assumes no transaction costs or trade barriers, and forms the microeconomic foundation for purchasing power parity theory.
